expectations.text: the agent should read skills/web-search/SKILL.md before searching; the skill defines the 5-movement loop and source hierarchy
the response should prefer primary sources (docs.rs, Rust RFCs, the Rust reference) over secondary aggregators (StackOverflow, blogs)
expectations.evidence: structure assertion without compare_args falls back to LLM judge

expectations.text: the response should cite ≥2 specific URLs (with stable identifiers like docs.rs/std/...) for the Rust async traits documentation
expectations.evidence: structure assertion without compare_args falls back to LLM judge

expectations.text: the agent should apply the 5-movement loop (frame -> reformulate -> inspect -> cross-reference -> stop/surface), not a single-pass search
expectations.evidence: The agent framed the topic ('this topic has evolved significantly… so I want primary sources'), issued 4 reformulated search queries, fetched/inspected 4 primary source pages, cross-referenced results (noting 'The 1.78 blog post didn't directly cover async traits… so I should note that the async fn in trait stabilization was actually in 1.75, not 1.78'), and then stopped to synthesize/surface findings — a clear 5-movement loop rather than a single-pass search.

expectations.text: the response should note the recency of sources (e.g. async-trait crate status, whether Rust 1.75+ stabilized native async traits) and surface limitations of the search (e.g. 'official std docs are sparse on this topic')
expectations.evidence: The agent explicitly cited recency anchors ('async fn in traits stabilized in 1.75, return type notation in 1.78'), fetched the docs.rs/async-trait crate page for current crate status, and surfaced a search limitation by correcting its own framing ('The 1.78 blog post didn't directly cover async traits (it was about diagnostic attributes, etc.)… I should not pretend the 1.78 post is the async traits one') before beginning synthesis.
